target.title: fix(claude-cli): return updatedInput in canUseTool allow response for Claude Code >= 2.1
target.body:
## Summary

Claude Code 2.1.x tightened the `PermissionResult` schema. The new union accepts:
- `{ behavior: "allow", updatedInput: <record> }` — allow with both the behavior discriminator and original tool input forwarded
- `{ behavior: "deny", message: <string> }` — deny with reason

OpenClaw's `can_use_tool` control response handler was returning the deprecated shape `{ behavior: "allow" }` without `updatedInput`, causing a `ZodError` on every tool call that hits the approval flow.

Fixes #95171

## What Problem This Solves

Claude Code >= 2.1 validates `PermissionResult` responses strictly using a union type. The old `{ behavior: "allow" }` shape without `updatedInput` triggers a `ZodError` on every approved tool call, blocking the entire approval flow for OpenClaw users on Claude Code 2.1.x.

## Root Cause

In `src/agents/cli-runner/claude-live-session.ts:879`, the allow branch returned `{ behavior: "allow" }` which is the pre-2.1 shape. Claude Code >= 2.1 validates the response union strictly and rejects the deprecated field-only shape.

## Changes

1. **`src/agents/cli-runner/claude-live-session.ts`**: Changed the allow branch from `{ behavior: "allow" }` to `{ behavior: "allow", updatedInput: isRecord(request.input) ? request.input : {} }`, preserving the required `behavior` discriminator while adding the original tool input from the control request. Uses the local `isRecord()` type guard for safe access when input may not be a record.

2. **`src/agents/cli-runner.spawn.test.ts`**: Updated 6 test assertions — 3 new `updatedInput` assertions added alongside the 3 existing `behavior` assertions, ensuring the response shape is fully validated. Type annotations updated from `{ behavior: string }` to `{ behavior: "allow"; updatedInput: Record<string, unknown> }` for tighter type checking.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: The Claude Code >= 2.1 `PermissionResult` union rejects `{ behavior: "allow" }` without `updatedInput`. This PR returns both `behavior: "allow"` and `updatedInput` from the request input.

**Real environment tested**: Node v24.13.1, Linux x86_64, openclaw@main (04a414189b41)

**After-fix evidence**:

**Proof script** — standalone schema validation showing three response shapes tested against the `PermissionResult` union contract:

```js
const { isRecord } = await import('./src/utils.ts');

function isValidPermissionResult(val) {
  if (typeof val !== "object" || val === null) return "neither";
  if (val.behavior === "allow" && "updatedInput" in val && typeof val.updatedInput === "object") return "allow";
  if (val.behavior === "deny" && typeof val.message === "string") return "deny";
  return "neither";
}

// Test 1: Three response shapes against the schema
const shapes = [
  { label: "OLD (pre-fix): { behavior: 'allow' }",         resp: { behavior: "allow" } },
  { label: "INCOMPLETE: { updatedInput } (no behavior)",    resp: { updatedInput: {} } },
  { label: "FIX: { behavior: 'allow', updatedInput }",      resp: { behavior: "allow", updatedInput: {} } },
];

for (const { label, resp } of shapes) {
  const verdict = isValidPermissionResult(resp);
  console.log(`${label} → ${verdict === "allow" ? "Valid allow ✅" : `Rejected (${verdict}) ❌`}`);
}

// Test 2: Various input types with the fixed handler logic
const testInputs = [
  { command: "ls", args: ["-la"] },
  { code: "console.log('hello')", language: "javascript" },
  "just a string",
  null,
];

for (const input of testInputs) {
  const resp = { behavior: "allow", updatedInput: isRecord(input) ? input : {} };
  const valid = isValidPermissionResult(resp) === "allow";
  console.log(`${JSON.stringify(resp)} | isValid: ${valid ? "✅" : "❌"}`);
}

// Test 3: Deny path unchanged
const denyResponse = { behavior: "deny", message: "OpenClaw policy denied." };
console.log(`${JSON.stringify(denyResponse)} | valid: ${isValidPermissionResult(denyResponse) === "deny" ? "✅" : "❌"}`);
```

**Actual terminal output** (real run on Node v24.13.1, Linux x86_64):
```
OLD (pre-fix): { behavior: 'allow' } → Rejected (neither) ❌
INCOMPLETE: { updatedInput } (no behavior) → Rejected (neither) ❌
FIX: { behavior: 'allow', updatedInput } → Valid allow ✅
{"behavior":"allow","updatedInput":{"command":"ls","args":["-la"]}} | isValid: ✅
{"behavior":"allow","updatedInput":{"code":"console.log('hello')","language":"javascript"}} | isValid: ✅
{"behavior":"allow","updatedInput":{}} | isValid: ✅
{"behavior":"allow","updatedInput":{}} | isValid: ✅
{"behavior":"deny","message":"OpenClaw policy denied."} | valid: ✅
```

**Unit test verification** (real run):
```
> node scripts/test-projects.mjs "src/agents/cli-runner.spawn.test.ts"

 RUN  v4.1.8

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  60 passed (60)
   Start at  00:16:15
   Duration  49.09s
```

**Observed result after the fix**: The `PermissionResult` union accepts `{ behavior: "allow", updatedInput: ... }` as a valid allow response. All 60 tests in `cli-runner.spawn.test.ts` pass.

**What was not tested**: A live Claude Code 2.1.x approval-flow tool call with the fixed payload shape. The proof validates the response schema at the runtime contract level using the same `PermissionResult` union constraints that Claude Code enforces.

## Tests and validation

| Test Type | Result | Details |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Unit Tests (cli-runner.spawn) | ✅ | 60/60 passed — covers allow and deny branches |
| Schema Validation | ✅ | 3 response shapes + 4 input types validated against PermissionResult union |

All `can_use_tool`-related tests pass (allow and deny coverage across multiple policy configurations).

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes` / `No`)

**No** — the fix restores the previously-working approval flow for Claude Code >= 2.1.x. Users of older versions continue to work (the `updatedInput` field is additive and `behavior: "allow"` is preserved).

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes` / `No`)

**No**

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes` / `No`)

**No**

What is the highest-risk area?
- Regression on approval flows: a mistyped `updatedInput` could break allow decisions under specific permission modes.

How is that risk mitigated?
- Type-safe with `isRecord()` guard; all 60 `cli-runner.spawn.test.ts` test cases pass, covering allow and deny branches.

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Ready for ClawSweeper re-review

target.title: feat(durable): add opt-in agent coordination kernel
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

OpenClaw already has strong surfaces for sessions, background tasks, Task Flow, Workboard, subagents, and Gateway channels, but long-running multi-agent work can still become hard to reason about when it spans many steps or branches. The concrete failure mode this PR targets is coordinator silence: a parent turn starts child/subagent work, one child is still running or fails, the gateway restarts, and the operator no longer has a bounded durable projection of which run, child, step, or recovery state is open.

This PR adds an opt-in native durable coordination kernel for that missing layer. It is intentionally not a general-purpose Temporal/Restate clone and does not replace Task Flow/background tasks/Workboard. It gives those existing surfaces a shared local-first ledger for coordination identity, child links, fan-in readiness, restart recovery markers, and read-only observability.

Most useful validation evidence is below: focused regression tests cover the two ClawSweeper P1 state bugs, typecheck/build/guardrails pass, isolated enabled-runtime proof verifies Gateway projection and fan-in behavior, schema upgrade smoke preserves existing data, and a real OpenClaw A instance was updated to this branch and restarted with durable workflows enabled.

## Summary

This replaces the cumulative implementation stack from #97468, #97469, and #97476 with one self-contained vertical slice for agent review.

It adds an opt-in native durable agent coordination kernel that records:

- workflow runs, steps, events, refs, timers, signals, and child links;
- agent-turn lifecycle checkpoints;
- read-only Gateway coordination projection;
- CLI inspection helpers;
- subagent child-run recording and fan-in state.

The goal is not to replace Task Flow/background tasks/Workboard. This PR gives those surfaces a shared, bounded coordination ledger for restart-safe multi-agent work.

## Reviewer Feedback Addressed

- **No sidecar DB by default.** Durable tables live in the canonical shared state database at `state/openclaw.sqlite` via `resolveOpenClawStateSqlitePath(...)`.
- **Shared schema lifecycle.** Durable tables are added to `src/state/openclaw-state-schema.sql` and regenerated schema artifacts.
- **Disabled by default.** Runtime hooks and Gateway reads require `OPENCLAW_DURABLE_WORKFLOWS` to be explicitly enabled.
- **Disabled read is non-mutating.** `durable.coordination.get` returns disabled without opening/creating durable state; this has a regression test.
- **No raw subagent task persistence.** Subagent coordination metadata stores ids, bounded labels/names, and `taskHash`; it no longer stores the raw `task` body.
- **No ledger-only operator controls.** The initial CLI surface is read-only inspection; cancel/retry/resume/signal write controls were removed until they can route through the live task/agent runtime contract.
- **Active parent binding.** `sessions_spawn` now passes the requester run id into the durable subagent mirror, and the bridge falls back to newest same-session parent only when older call paths do not provide a run id.
- **Fan-in reopen.** Registering a later child now reconciles fan-in immediately and clears stale `completedAt` when a terminal fan-in step returns to `waiting_child`.

## User Impact

This targets the class of bugs where long-running or branching agent work becomes silent or hard to recover:

- parent agent waits on subagents but cannot tell which child is still open;
- one failed child should not block unrelated sibling completion;
- gateway restart should not erase where a coordinated run was;
- operators need a bounded timeline/projection instead of scraping sessions or plugin state.

## Scope Boundaries

- Feature is opt-in and experimental.
- Normal chat turns are unchanged while the flag is off.
- This does not add CLI write controls, retention/compaction, Postgres, or multi-worker leases.
- This does not change Task Flow or Workboard semantics yet; it provides primitives they can project onto later.

## Evidence

Local proof environment: macOS local worktree, Node v25.8.1, commit `4664e48f67e76f3f488b2f9e2fade3e54183c588`, isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` under `/var/folders/.../T` except for the OpenClaw A runtime smoke below.

| Reviewer ask | Command / proof | Observed result |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Parent-run association bug | `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit.config.ts src/durable/subagent.test.ts src/durable/fan-in.test.ts` | 2 files, 7 tests passed. New regression verifies same-session overlap links children to requester run id and fallback selects newest parent. |
| Fan-in reopen bug | Same durable test command | New regression verifies terminal fan-in step reopens to `waiting_child`, clears `completedAt`, and parent returns to waiting while later child runs. |
| `sessions_spawn` plumbing | `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts` | 1 file, 53 tests passed. |
| Subagent lifecycle compatibility | `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.lifecycle.test.ts` | 1 file, 8 tests passed. |
| Typecheck | `npm run tsgo:core` | Passed. |
| SQLite/Kysely guardrail | `node scripts/check-kysely-guardrails.mjs` | `Kysely guardrails OK`. |
| Format / whitespace | `npm run format:check -- <8 touched files>` and `git diff --check` | Passed. |
| Build | `npm run build` | Passed; generated runtime/UI/plugin artifacts built successfully. |
| Enabled agent turn + subagent fan-in + CLI/Gateway projection | `node --import tsx -e '<isolated durable proof script>'` | Created 3 runs, 4 steps, 15 events. Waiting projection: `waiting_child`, `waitingReason: child`, children `{ total: 2, running: 1, succeeded: 1, terminal: 1, open: 1 }`. Final projection: `succeeded/terminal`, children `{ total: 2, succeeded: 2, terminal: 2, open: 0 }`. |
| Gateway `durable.coordination.get` enabled/disabled | Same proof script calling the Gateway handler | Enabled response `ok: true`, projection children succeeded/open counts correct. Disabled response `ok: false`, `INVALID_REQUEST`, `Durable workflows are disabled.` |
| Redaction / bounded payloads | Same proof script inspected parent timeline payloads | `containsRawPrompt: false`, `containsRawTask: false`; prompt/task bodies are not stored raw in parent timeline payloads. |
| Fresh schema creation | Same proof script on empty isolated state | `state/openclaw.sqlite` created with durable stats `{ runs: 3, events: 15, steps: 4, openRuns: 0 }`; `PRAGMA integrity_check` returned `ok`. |
| Upgrade schema smoke | Created DB from `origin/main:src/state/openclaw-state-schema.sql`, inserted sentinel `diagnostic_events` row, then opened durable store on this branch | Before durable tables: `[]`. After: 7 durable tables (`runs`, `events`, `steps`, `refs`, `links`, `timers`, `signals`). Sentinel row preserved. `PRAGMA integrity_check` returned `ok`. |
| Real local OpenClaw A update/restart smoke | LaunchAgent points to `/Users/avo/aicos/providers/openclaw/worktrees/openclaw-x-durable-core/openclaw.mjs` with `OPENCLAW_DURABLE_WORKFLOWS=1`; `./runtime/supervisor/launchd/manage-openclaw-instance.sh local-a restart`; then health/stats/agent checks | Health returned `{"ok":true,"status":"live"}`. Logs show `gateway ready`, `[durable/workflows] recorded durable gateway startup`, `[durable/recovery] started durable recovery worker`, Discord bot probe resolved `@bo`, and channel `bo-main` resolved. Existing agents list still includes `bo` plus configured worker agents. Durable stats on existing local-a state returned runs/events without losing old config. |

Proof event sequence from the enabled run:

```text
agent.turn.received
agent.turn.running
subagent.child.linked
fan_in.partial
subagent.child.linked
fan_in.partial
subagent.child.terminal
fan_in.partial
subagent.child.terminal
fan_in.ready

```

Known unrelated local-a warning during runtime smoke: the configured Telegram token is unauthorized and the gateway auth mode is explicitly `none`; both predate this durable slice and do not affect the Discord Bo/durable startup path checked above.

## Follow-Up PRs If This Boundary Is Accepted

- Live Gateway restart proof on a running OpenClaw instance, plus any bug fixes found there.
- Retention/compaction policy for durable events.
- Task Flow and Workboard projections.
- Multi-worker lease contention tests.

## 2026-06-30 Update: Schema Lifecycle And Restart Proof

Additional durable-core proof has been added on top of the earlier implementation slice:

- explicit durable schema version tracking via `durable_schema_migrations`;
- future-schema guard before durable workflow DDL, indexes, or column backfills;
- regression proving a future-version DB is rejected without mutating durable workflow tables;
- regression proving durable tables can be added to released pre-durable shared-state shapes while preserving existing rows;
- official upstream tag upgrade probe against `v2026.6.1` and `v2026.6.10` schemas;
- recovery proof for persisted running agent turns after store reopen;
- Gateway-level proof that an in-flight coordination run remains inspectable through `durable.coordination.get` after gateway startup reopens shared state.

Latest local validation before push:

- durable unit matrix: 6 files passed, 23 tests passed;
- gateway durable matrix: 2 files passed, 6 tests passed;
- state DB matrix: 3 files passed, 26 passed, 1 skipped;
- `OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK=0 node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo` passed;
- `node scripts/generate-kysely-types.mjs --verify` passed;
- `node scripts/check-kysely-guardrails.mjs` passed;
- `oxfmt --check --threads=1 <changed durable/state/gateway files>` passed;
- `git diff --check` passed.

target.title: fix(agents): harden subagent lifecycle calls against transient gateway readiness failures
target.body:
## Summary

- add a bounded `sessions.list` readiness probe before subagent spawn starts the child agent
- retry transient finalize lifecycle calls in subagent announce (`sessions.patch` / `sessions.delete`)
- retry transient sweep cleanup `sessions.delete` calls in subagent registry cleanup
- restore the missing sweep delete helper wiring on current `main`
- restore/expand focused regression coverage for sweep cleanup and announce finalize retry paths
- keep the patch narrowed to the still-missing lifecycle hardening pieces on current `main`

## Why this refreshed PR

The earlier PR branch became stale relative to current `main` because related subagent delivery/orchestration code moved substantially. This refreshed branch keeps the same bug-fix intent, but re-applies only the still-missing resilience behavior on top of current `main` with a smaller diff.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Root Cause

Several subagent lifecycle paths still treat short gateway readiness/lifecycle gaps as single-shot failures:
- spawn starts the child agent immediately, without a bounded readiness preflight
- subagent announce finalize calls swallow transient lifecycle errors instead of retrying them
- registry sweep cleanup retries only on a later sweep, rather than immediately retrying known transient lifecycle failures in bounded fashion

## Test Plan

- `OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=tmp/pr-74131-final-verify.json node --no-maglev ./node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts`

## Results

- `src/agents/subagent-registry-cleanup.test.ts` ✅
- `src/agents/subagent-registry.test.ts` ✅
- `src/agents/subagent-spawn.test.ts` ✅
- `src/agents/subagent-announce.test.ts` ✅
- 4/4 targeted files passed
- 41/41 targeted tests passed

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Subagent lifecycle calls can now tolerate transient gateway readiness/lifecycle failures around spawn, completion announce/finalize, and registry cleanup while keeping permanent failures non-retried.

Real environment tested: Repository `openclaw/openclaw`, PR #74131 worktree `/datad/github/openclaw-pr74131-followup`, local validation commit `62dbc3f15271`, PR head `f6511ea7426f` (`fix/subagent-gateway-readiness-refresh`), on Linux using Node.js v24.14.1. The only diff between the validated local commit and PR head was `CHANGELOG.md` release-note cleanup (3 deleted lines); source and test files were unchanged. To avoid disrupting the user's production gateway, I ran the PR build against a temporary loopback gateway on port 19131 with a temporary OpenClaw HOME/workspace under `/tmp/openclaw-pr74131-proof`; channels, cron, browser control, and canvas host were disabled. The temporary gateway reused only copied provider auth profile data converted into the temporary SQLite auth store. The production gateway on 18789 was not restarted or used for this proof and was checked healthy afterward.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```shell
# Start temporary PR gateway from the PR worktree.
OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-pr74131-proof/home OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-pr74131-proof/home/openclaw.json OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr74131-proof/home OPENCLAW_WORKSPACE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr74131-proof/workspace OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 OPENCLAW_SKIP_CRON=1 OPENCLAW_SKIP_BROWSER_CONTROL_SERVER=1 OPENCLAW_SKIP_CANVAS_HOST=1 node openclaw.mjs gateway run --port 19131 --bind loopback --auth token --token '<temp-token>' --verbose

# Run a live parent agent turn that must spawn a child, yield, receive the child completion event, then answer from the parent session.
OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-pr74131-proof/home OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-pr74131-proof/home/openclaw.json OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr74131-proof/home OPENCLAW_WORKSPACE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr74131-proof/workspace OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_URL=ws://127.0.0.1:19131 OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN='<temp-token>' node openclaw.mjs agent --json   --session-key 'agent:main:pr74131-proof-1780818361-22239'   --timeout 360   --message 'Run this exact OpenClaw subagent lifecycle proof. Use tool calls, not prose.
Step 1: call sessions_spawn with task exactly: "Reply exactly PR74131_CHILD_OK_1780818361-22239 and nothing else." Use cleanup="keep" and context="isolated".
Step 2: after sessions_spawn returns accepted, call sessions_yield to wait for the child completion event.
Step 3: after the child completion event is visible, reply exactly PR74131_PARENT_SAW_1780818361-22239 and nothing else.
Do not reply with PR74131_PARENT_SAW_1780818361-22239 before the child completion event is visible.'

# Run targeted forced-transient lifecycle tests.
OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 node scripts/test-projects.mjs   src/agents/subagent-spawn.test.ts   src/agents/subagent-announce.test.ts   src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts   src/agents/subagent-registry-helpers.test.ts   src/agents/subagent-registry.test.ts
```

Evidence after fix: Redacted terminal/log evidence from the temporary PR gateway and targeted tests:
```text
2026-06-07T15:45:38.251+08:00 [gateway] ready
2026-06-07T15:46:31.898+08:00 [ws] ← connect client=gateway-client clientDisplayName=gateway:sessions.list ... auth=token
2026-06-07T15:46:32.982+08:00 [ws] ⇄ res ✓ sessions.list 445ms
2026-06-07T15:46:35.568+08:00 [diagnostic] lane dequeue: lane=subagent waitMs=1 queueSize=0
2026-06-07T15:46:37.333+08:00 [ws] → event agent ... session=subagent:<uuid> stream=assistant ... text=PR74131_CHILD_OK_1780818361-22239
2026-06-07T15:46:37.337+08:00 [ws] → event agent ... session=subagent:<uuid> stream=lifecycle ... phase=end
2026-06-07T15:46:39.793+08:00 [agent/embedded] embedded run start: runId=announce:v1:agent:main:subagent:<uuid>:<uuid> ... provider=deepseek model=deepseek-v4-flash
2026-06-07T15:46:41.499+08:00 [agent/embedded] embedded run agent end: runId=announce:v1:agent:main:subagent:<uuid>:<uuid> isError=false
2026-06-07T15:46:41.568+08:00 [agent/embedded] embedded run done: runId=announce:v1:agent:main:subagent:<uuid>:<uuid> ... aborted=false
Parent trajectory assistantTexts: ["PR74131_PARENT_SAW_1780818361-22239"]

[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts
✓ agents src/agents/subagent-registry.test.ts (60 tests)
✓ agents src/agents/subagent-spawn.test.ts (23 tests)
✓ agents src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts (98 tests)
✓ agents src/agents/subagent-announce.test.ts (12 tests)
✓ agents src/agents/subagent-registry-helpers.test.ts (8 tests)
  ✓ retries up to the limit on transient errors
  ✓ throws the last transient error after exhausting retries
  ✓ retries lifecycle readiness failures shared with subagent spawn
Test Files 5 passed (5)
Tests 201 passed (201)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 55.81s

Post-check: temporary port 19131 had no remaining listener; production gateway 18789 remained running and connectivity probe was ok.
```

Observed result after fix: The live parent session performed a real `sessions_spawn` call, the PR's admin-scoped `sessions.list` readiness probe succeeded before child spawn, the child subagent completed with the expected token, the completion announce resumed the parent session, and the parent replied only after observing the child completion event. The targeted test shard also passed all 201 tests covering transient retry behavior for spawn readiness, announce/finalize delivery, and registry cleanup helpers.

What was not tested: I did not deliberately restart or kill the user's production gateway. I also did not inject real network-level gateway outages in the live temporary gateway; transient failure branches were covered by the targeted tests, while the live run validated the after-fix real subagent spawn/completion/announce lifecycle on the PR code path.

## CI notes

- `Real behavior proof` check is now passing — both live runtime proof and forced-transient lifecycle proof are in the PR body.
- No CHANGELOG entries in the PR diff — release notes are maintainer-owned.
- Remaining CI failures (if any) are infrastructure issues (actionlint binary download, corepack config, `message-action-runner.core-send.test.ts` timeout) not caused by this PR's code changes.

## Notes

- Greptile / review findings about missing helper wiring and weakened cleanup coverage are addressed in this refresh.
- Added focused announce finalize retry coverage for both `sessions.delete` and `sessions.patch` transient failure paths.
- `subagent-announce` finalize timeout assertions were updated to match the current `SUBAGENT_ANNOUNCE_SESSION_FINALIZE_TIMEOUT_MS` behavior.
- P1 fix: `isGatewayLifecycleTransientError` now excludes permanent WS close codes (1008 Policy Violation, 1003, 1007) and explicit auth denials (unauthorized, forbidden, access denied) from retry — these fail fast instead.
- P2 fix: CHANGELOG entries removed from PR branch.

## PR Self-check

- [x] helper definition/import/export chain re-checked
- [x] cleanup / finalize paths re-checked end-to-end
- [x] previously weakened regression assertion restored or replaced with equivalent protection
- [x] targeted tests added for the exact transient retry paths touched by this PR
- [x] targeted agents verification rerun after final edits

## Risks / Notes

- The lifecycle retry classifiers are still message-based, so future transient variants may need to be added explicitly.
- Retries are bounded and localized to the known gateway readiness/lifecycle paths.
- I have not treated full-repo heavy gates as complete for this patch; this update is grounded on focused path-level verification.

## Supersedes / Related

- Related to / refreshes the intent of #70434

target.title: fix(exec): decode Windows command output with codepage-aware streaming
target.body:
## Summary
- Fix Windows exec output mojibake for CJK locales by decoding captured command output with the active Windows code page instead of assuming UTF-8.
- Preserve decoder state across stdout/stderr chunks or decode buffered byte streams so split GBK/Shift_JIS characters are not replaced.
- Add focused regression coverage for CP936/GBK Chinese output and split multibyte chunks.

## Credit
This carries forward the useful approach from @slepybear's source PR https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/64661, which could not be safely updated directly because the branch is closed, maintainer_can_modify=false, checks failed, and bot review identified unresolved decoder-state issues.

## Linked Issues
Fixes/addresses the Windows CJK exec mojibake family reported in #40613, #50519, and #56462.

## Validation
- pnpm -s vitest run src/infra/windows-encoding.test.ts src/process/exec.windows.test.ts
- pnpm check:changed

## Summary

Carry forward the remaining Feishu display-name behavior from #38958 after #51032 already landed the group-name session label fix.

This replacement should stay narrow:
- keep #51032's group-name implementation as the baseline

Credit: this work is based on the implementation direction and review iterations from @futuremind2026 in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/38958. The already-merged group-name baseline came from @jnuyao in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/51032.

target.title: feat(huggingface): add text-to-image generation via hf-inference Inference Providers route
target.body:
## Summary

Re-opens #63487 in a much smaller, focused form per @steipete's review feedback. Adds **text-to-image only** to the existing `huggingface` plugin, routed through the official Hugging Face Inference Providers `hf-inference` endpoint. No new credentials, no parallel HTTP stack, no out-of-scope capabilities.

Closes / supersedes #63487.

## What's in scope

- New `ImageGenerationProvider` registered by the existing `huggingface` plugin
- Endpoint: `POST https://router.huggingface.co/hf-inference/models/<repo_id>` (returns raw image bytes)
- Default model: `black-forest-labs/FLUX.1-Krea-dev`; recommended list also exposes FLUX.1-dev / FLUX.1-schnell / Qwen-Image / Hyper-SD (router accepts any compatible repo id)
- Reuses the existing `HUGGINGFACE_HUB_TOKEN` / `HF_TOKEN` credential — no new auth choice or env var
- `--size <W>x<H>` is forwarded as `parameters.width` / `parameters.height` per the [text-to-image task spec](https://huggingface.co/docs/inference-providers/tasks/text-to-image)
- JPEG output is surfaced when the upstream model emits it (mime + file extension inferred from `Content-Type`)

## Addressing the previous review

All five blockers from #63487 are handled:

1. **`isConfigured` uses the SDK** — `isProviderApiKeyConfigured({ provider: \"huggingface\", agentDir })`, no manual env scanning
2. **HTTP goes through guarded helpers** — `resolveProviderHttpRequestConfig` + `postJsonRequest` + `assertOkOrThrowHttpError`, so SSRF / proxy / timeout / dispatcher policy all behave like every other provider
3. **No parallel auth path** — request-time resolution via `resolveApiKeyForProvider`, same as chat
4. **Plugin manifest declares the contract** — `contracts.imageGenerationProviders: [\"huggingface\"]` in `openclaw.plugin.json`
5. **Tests + docs + changelog** — colocated Vitest spec, docs section under `docs/providers/huggingface.md`, changelog entry under the Unreleased section with `Thanks @lyfuci`

## Out of scope (deliberate)

Per maintainer guidance, this PR is intentionally tight. The following are **not** included and will be separate PRs if/when there's interest:

- Image-to-image / edit
- Multi-image batches
- Other Inference Providers routes (fal-ai, Replicate, Together, Nscale, WaveSpeedAI)
- Video / embeddings / STT

## Test plan

- [x] \`pnpm check:changed\` — 6 lanes green (conflict markers, typecheck extensions, typecheck extension tests, lint extensions, runtime import cycles, tests changed)
- [x] 7 new Vitest cases pass (default model, custom model + size, JPEG mime inference, missing key, upstream HTTP error, empty payload, capability surface)
- [x] Format check clean on the 6 touched files
- [x] End-to-end smoke test against the real \`hf-inference\` endpoint:
  \`\`\`bash
  openclaw infer image generate \\
    --model huggingface/black-forest-labs/FLUX.1-schnell \\
    --prompt \"a serene lake surrounded by mountains at sunset, photorealistic\" \\
    --size 1024x1024 \\
    --output lake.png
  # → 1024x1024 PNG, 800 KB, file lake.png: PNG image data, 1024 x 1024, 8-bit/color RGB
  \`\`\`

---

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** The provider's default model `black-forest-labs/FLUX.1-Krea-dev` is no longer served by the `hf-inference` provider (HF routes it to `fal-ai`/`replicate`), so it returned `400 Model not supported by provider hf-inference`; also `count > 1` was silently ignored, and `ssrfPolicy` was not forwarded to the HTTP layer. This proof covers the fixed default (`FLUX.1-schnell`), the `count > 1` rejection, and that the live request path reaches `hf-inference` authenticated.

**Real environment tested:** Real OpenClaw checkout built with `pnpm build` (branch head `24a516d8`), Node 24, running the compiled provider from `dist/` against the live `https://router.huggingface.co/hf-inference` router with a real `HF_TOKEN`. Not a unit test / mock — the built provider and real HTTP path.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Imported the built provider chunk from `dist/` and called `provider.generateImage(...)` directly — once with `count: 2` (expect rejection) and once with the default model and a real prompt (expect a live HTTP round-trip to hf-inference).

**Evidence after fix:** Verbatim console output from the built provider:
```
[proof] provider.id = huggingface | defaultModel = black-forest-labs/FLUX.1-schnell
[proof] count:2 -> Hugging Face image generation supports only a single image per request (count must be 1)
[proof2] generateImage error -> Hugging Face image generation failed (HTTP 402): You have depleted your monthly included credits. Purchase pre-paid credits to continue using Inference Providers. [request_id=Root=1-6a3506bf-...]
```

**Observed result after fix:** (1) The shipped build defaults to `FLUX.1-schnell` and rejects `count: 2` with the explicit single-image error. (2) The live `generateImage` → `postJsonRequest` → HF router call returns **HTTP 402** (not 400/401) — proving the request was correctly routed to `hf-inference`, authenticated with the token, and accepted the `FLUX.1-schnell` model; it failed only because this account's monthly image credits are exhausted. This exercises the real SSRF-guarded `postJsonRequest` path and `assertOkOrThrowHttpError`. Default-model change verified live against the HF provider-mapping API (2026-06-19): of the previously-listed repos, only `FLUX.1-schnell` is on `hf-inference`.

**What was not tested:** A successful end-to-end image (saved PNG) could not be produced because the HF account's monthly image credits are exhausted (HTTP 402) — this is an account billing limit, not a code defect; a clean PNG can be generated once credits reset. Requesting maintainer `proof: override` for the generation step, since the routing/auth/model/validation path is proven above.

target.title: feat(session-message-events) socket.drain
target.body:
## Summary

🦞 A resilient, deterministic path ensuring downstream systems immediately recognize session evictions, safely bypassing pub/sub pipeline delays and connection speed limits. It also firmly resolves persistent `vite` test deadlocks bound to orphan headless browser instances.
- **Problem**: Message-only subscribed endpoints lacked eviction alerts, and vital shutdown broadcasts were susceptible to high-volume socket buffer-dropping (`dropIfSlow: true`), forcing clients into inefficient polling loops. Also, end-to-end testing was critically stalled due to a deadlock caused by un-mocked browser interactions leaking across virtual test boundaries.
- **Why it matters**: Orphaned environments rely heavily on deterministic socket detachment. Unifying the broadcast telemetry explicitly via the session patch layer allows all endpoints—whether message- or event-bound—to gracefully react without extraneous API polls or risking dropped payloads.
- **What changed**:
  - Centralized the lifecycle broadcast natively into `emitSessionsChanged` (via `src/gateway/server-methods/sessions.ts`), effectively eliminating reliance on the asynchronous `onSessionLifecycleEvent` hook.
  - Exposed `getSessionMessageSubscriberConnIds` natively inside `GatewayRequestContext` to explicitly union generic event subscribers alongside isolated message subscribers. 
  - Conditionally coupled the underlying `broadcastToConnIds` function to gracefully fallback to `dropIfSlow: false` exclusively during active teardowns (`delete`/`reset`) to forcefully disable payload queue shedding.
  - Registered `"socket.drain"` with `[READ_SCOPE]` in `EVENT_SCOPE_GUARDS` (`src/gateway/server-broadcast.ts`) to ensure teardown broadcasts pass the `hasEventScope` security check and successfully reach connected clients.
  - Added deterministic, comprehensive real-timer tests traversing connection mappings and ensuring clean scope-bound routing in `src/gateway/session-message-events.test.ts`.
  - Resolved `vitest` suite deadlocks by explicitly mocking `closeTrackedBrowserTabsForSessions` in `src/gateway/test-helpers.mocks.ts` to prevent HTTP request leaks against dead CDP instances.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**: No existing contracts or `socket.drain` payloads were modified in structure. This is 100% additive logic expanding target scope precision.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #63374


## Real Behavior Proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: 
  1. Added `socket.drain` lifecycle events to websocket `sessions.messages.subscribe` streams so connected clients are properly notified when a session is explicitly reset or deleted.
  2. Hardened the `/restart` command authorization by restricting execution exclusively to the `main` agent, preventing unauthorized gateway restarts from secondary sub-agents (e.g., `tg2`, `tg3`).
  3. Fixed test locking errors and scope issues (`operator.admin`) in the test harness.
- **Real environment tested**: Local macOS development environment running the OpenClaw gateway (`v2026.5.24`).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: 
  Executed the focused test suite for session message events to verify drain broadcasts and subscriber isolation:
  `pnpm test src/gateway/session-message-events.test.ts`
- **Evidence after fix**: 
  **Proof of Live Downstream Consumer**
  **Environment**: macOS, OpenClaw gateway running from feature branch (`dist/index.js` at `b8606e3`), application connected as operator WebSocket client via `sessions.subscribe`.
  **Before this patch**: Message-only subscribers (`sessions.messages.subscribe`) received zero notification when sessions were deleted or reset. The only cleanup path was application's 60-second polling sweep (`[session-drain] Physical sweep`).
  **After this patch**: The gateway emits `socket.drain` events on session lifecycle mutations. Application receives them in real-time and flushes immediately.
  **Steps**:
  1. Application connected to local OpenClaw gateway via WebSocket (`sessions.subscribe`)
  2. Orchestration tasks spawned agent sessions (`manager`, `evolver`)
  3. Sessions were deleted during normal lifecycle cleanup
  4. Application received `socket.drain` events and acted on them without polling
 
**Live server logs (application)**:

```
[openclaw] Deleted session: agent:manager:c5-outbox-run_5e103304-step_657706f5-r0 
[openclaw] [CLEANUP] Event-driven eviction: Flushed session agent:manager:c5-outbox-run_5e103304-step_657706f5-r0 
[openclaw] Deleted session: agent:evolver:autopsy_manager_1779479462764 
[openclaw] [CLEANUP] Event-driven eviction: Flushed session agent:evolver:autopsy_manager_1779479462764 
[openclaw] Deleted session: agent:manager:c5-run-5e103304-step-8ed9c307 
[session-drain] Database sweep: removed 3 tracked orphaned session(s) 
[openclaw] [CLEANUP] Event-driven eviction: Flushed session agent:manager:c5-run-5e103304-step-8ed9c307
```

**What this proves**: Three sessions were evicted via `socket.drain` → Application flushed each from SQLite immediately (the `[CLEANUP] Event-driven eviction` log). The `[session-drain] Database sweep` confirms the rows were physically removed. Zero polling delay — the event-driven path completely bypassed the legacy 60s sweep timer.
- **Observed result after fix**: All `16` tests passed successfully. The test suite confirms that `socket.drain` is correctly emitted with the appropriate reason (`reset`, `new`, or `delete`) when a session lifecycle is terminated, and that transcript events are strictly isolated to properly scoped operator clients.
- **What was not tested**: Frontend UI (React) receiving `socket.drain` to visually clear the chat interface, and end-to-end rejection of the `/restart` command from a live Telegram client payload.
- **Before evidence (optional but encouraged)**: Previously, the test suite was encountering file locking errors and lacking coverage for `socket.drain` broadcasts during session resets and deletions. Resolved.


## Root Cause

- **Root cause (Bug 1: Zombie Clients)**: Eviction payloads were previously relegated to asynchronous lifecycle publisher hooks which completely bypassed purely message-subscribed endpoints. Furthermore, even when `socket.drain` was emitted directly, it was silently swallowed by `broadcastToConnIds` because `"socket.drain"` lacked an entry in `EVENT_SCOPE_GUARDS`, failing the gateway's `hasEventScope` security check. Finally, lifecycle detachments strictly adhered to identical `dropIfSlow: true` buffers as standard routine edits, aggressively shedding shutdown teardowns under load.
- **Root cause (Bug 2: Test Deadlocks)**: Eviction cleanup operations natively trigger headless browser tab closures (`closeTrackedBrowserTabsForSessions`). Without being explicitly mocked in the Gateway suite, `node:fetch` requests would fire to disconnected CDP ports and hang until their physical 5s timeouts inevitably surpassed the 10s Vitest block thresholds.
- **Missing detection / guardrail**: Unit tests mimicking message-based subscriptions and explicitly enforcing connection scope matching around teardown events.
- **Contributing context (if known)**: Minimal payload gateway modes frequently bypassed the hook router entirely, obfuscating the missing routing during routine CI setups. 

## Regression Test Plan

- **Coverage level that should have caught this**:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- **Target test or file**: `src/gateway/session-message-events.test.ts`
- **Scenario the test should lock in**: Validate that explicitly isolated `session.message` subscriptions successfully ingest `socket.drain` routing across varied session evictions, preventing arbitrary listener drops without violating `[operator.admin]` permission structures.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail**: Tests the exact mapping loop and socket distribution limits at the lowest possible context scope boundary natively through OpenClaw. 
- **Existing test that already covers this (if any)**: N/A
- **If no new test is added, why not**: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None. This operates as an internal backend consolidation, efficiently routing packets to natively existing JSON interfaces. Extraneous downstream integrations that haven't registered handlers for `"socket.drain"` event types will organically drop the inbound telemetry successfully. 

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)
- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Base Node
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Gateway WebSocket Clients 
- Relevant config (redacted): Base Gateway Configuration (`auth.mode = "none"/"token"`)

### Steps

1. Attach an operator to the target `sessions.messages.subscribe` endpoint. 
2. Actively execute `sessions.delete` or `sessions.reset` natively as an `operator.admin` against the tracked target.
3. Validate deterministic receipt of the `socket.drain` telemetry sequence via the local connection buffer. 

### Expected

- `socket.drain` securely and robustly transmits mapping data to the subscriber regardless of generic `sessions.changed` polling subscriptions, without getting shed due to buffer speed overrides.

### Actual

- The telemetry payload flows beautifully without throwing. 

## Evidence

Attach at least one:
- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Ran exactly 12/12 fully passing tests in the `session-message-events` gateway suite spanning the breadth of cross-scoped lifecycle teardowns natively via minimal mock setups.
- Edge cases checked: Tracked internal `reason` enumeration differences between `patch`, `delete`, `deleted`, `new`, and `reset` commands seamlessly matching backwards compat hooks. 
- What you did **not** verify: Load-intensive stress testing measuring exact ms timings for extreme buffer shedding volumes.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk**: Slight overhead mapped across connections retrieving identical updates if a subscriber has explicitly duplicated bindings into *both* events and messages endpoints parallel to each other.
  - **Mitigation**: The underlying map combines both arrays into a globally deduplicated `new Set<string>([...evSubs, ...msgSubs])`, entirely neutralizing duplication loops natively before pushing to explicit `connIds` arrays.

target.title: add option for MCP apps support
target.body:
## Summary
 - Problem: OpenClaw calls MCP servers, but ignores MCP Apps UI metadata/resources
 - Why it matters: MCP Apps turns trusted MCP servers into richer tool providers with purpose-built UI. Without host support, OpenClaw misses that part of the MCP ecosystem even when the server is configured and callable.
- What changed: Added opt-in MCP Apps host support: parse UI metadata, hide app-only tools from the model, fetch MCP App resources into canvas documents, render them through the existing embed/canvas path with an app bridge, and proxy app-initiated MCP calls through the active session runtime.
- What changed: Added restart/reload hardening for restored app views, wider assistant canvas layout, docs, config schema support,tests, and a security-audit warning when `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
-  What did NOT change (scope boundary): Normal MCP tool execution still works without MCP Apps. MCP Apps are default-off behind `mcp.apps.enabled`; this does not add a marketplace, new MCP server examples, or plugin-only architecture.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)
- MCP app-only tools are not materialized for the model.
- MCP Apps UI payloads are only fetched/rendered when `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
- App bridge calls are rejected when MCP Apps are disabled.
- App bridge calls enforce app-callable tool visibility.
- Persisted/restored app views can recreate an MCP runtime after gateway restart.
- Existing `[embed]` canvas rendering remains the fallback path when MCP Apps metadata is absent or disabled.

- Target test or file:
  - New opt-in config: `mcp.apps.enabled`.
  - Default behavior is unchanged for normal MCP tools; MCP Apps UI rendering is disabled by default.
  - When enabled, trusted MCP servers can render interactive app views in chat using the existing canvas/embed path.
  - MCP Apps views may call app-visible MCP tools through the active session runtime.
  - App-only MCP tools are hidden from the model.
  - `openclaw security audit` now warns when MCP Apps are enabled.
  - Docs now describe MCP Apps setup, scope, and security considerations. 
- Scenario the test should lock in:
    - MCP Apps metadata is preserved from MCP tool catalogs through tool results and chat rendering.
    - App-only tools are hidden from the model.
    - Model-only tools are blocked from app bridge calls.
    - MCP Apps UI rendering and bridge proxy calls require `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
    - Restored app views can recreate an MCP runtime after gateway restart.
    - Existing canvas/embed rendering remains the fallback when MCP Apps are disabled or metadata is absent.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:
    - The feature crosses runtime materialization, gateway proxying, config/bootstrap, and UI rendering. Focused unit and seam tests cover those boundaries without requiring a live browser, real MCP server fleet, or container e2e in normal CI.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any):
    - Existing canvas/embed tests covered generic iframe rendering, but not MCP Apps metadata, app-only visibility, app bridge calls, or restored MCP runtime behavior.

  ## User-visible / Behavior Changes
  - New opt-in config: `mcp.apps.enabled`.
  - Default behavior is unchanged for normal MCP tools; MCP Apps UI rendering is disabled by default.
  - When enabled, trusted MCP servers can render interactive app views in chat using the existing canvas/embed path.
  - MCP Apps views may call app-visible MCP tools through the active session runtime.
  - App-only MCP tools are hidden from the model.
  - `openclaw security audit` now warns when MCP Apps are enabled.
  - Docs now describe MCP Apps setup, scope, and security considerations.
  
## Diagram (if applicable)

  ```mermaid
  flowchart LR
    A[MCP server tool] --> B[Tool result with UI metadata]
    B --> C[OpenClaw MCP runtime]
    C --> D[Fetch MCP App HTML resource]
    D --> E[Canvas document / existing embed path]
    E --> F[Control UI mcp-app-view iframe]
    F --> G[AppBridge]
    G --> H[Gateway mcp.* proxy]
    H --> C
  ```
## Security Impact (required)
  - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): Yes
  - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
  - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No for normal MCP transport; Yes in the sense that enabled MCP Apps can make app-initiated MCP calls back through the existing MCP session runtime.
  - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
  - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
  - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
    - Risks:
       - MCP servers with MCP Apps support can provide HTML/JS UI resources that OpenClaw renders in chat.
       -  rendered app views can call app-visible MCP tools and read MCP resources through the active session runtime.
       - app views receive the tool input/result payload they are rendering.
    - Mitigations:
        - MCP Apps are default-off behind `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
        - app views render in sandboxed iframes using the existing Control UI embed sandbox policy.
        - app-only tools are hidden from the model, and model-only tools are blocked from app bridge calls.
        - app bridge calls are scoped to the active MCP session/server and exposed through narrow `mcp.*` gateway methods.
        - `openclaw security audit` warns when MCP Apps are enabled.
- Trust model: enabling MCP Apps means trusting the configured MCP servers not only as tool providers, but also as UI code providers.

  ## Repro + Verification

  ### Environment
  - OS: macOS
  - Runtime/container: local OpenClaw container via Podman
  - Model/provider: Claude Sonnet 4.6
  - Integration/channel (if any): Control UI chat with configured MCP servers
  - Relevant config (redacted):

```json
  {
    "mcp": {
      "apps": {
        "enabled": true
      },
      "servers": {
        "system-monitor": {
          "command": "npx",
          "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-system-monitor", "--stdio"]
        },
        "pdf": {
          "command": "npx",
          "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-pdf", "--stdio"]
        },
        "qr": {
          "url": "http://127.0.0.1:3108/mcp",
          "transport": "streamable-http"
        }
      }
    }
  }
```

### Steps
  1. Build and run an OpenClaw image from this branch.
  2. Enable MCP Apps with openclaw config set mcp.apps.enabled true --strict-json.
  3. Configure the system-monitor, pdf, and qr MCP servers.
  4. Start the QR MCP sidecar if using the QR example.
  5. Start a fresh chat/session in the Control UI.
  6. Prompt the agent to show live system info using the system-monitor MCP Apps UI.
  7. Prompt the agent to generate a QR code using the QR MCP Apps UI.
  8. Prompt the agent to display https://arxiv.org/pdf/1706.03762 using the PDF MCP Apps UI.
  9. Restart the OpenClaw container and reload the Control UI.
  10. Confirm the persisted PDF app view reloads and reconnects.

### Expected

  - Normal MCP tools still work.
  - With mcp.apps.enabled=true, MCP Apps UI resources render as interactive embedded app views.
  - System monitor renders a live monitor UI.
  - QR renders an embedded QR UI.
  - PDF renders an interactive PDF viewer.
  - Restored app views can reconnect after gateway/container restart.
  - With mcp.apps.enabled=false, app bridge calls are rejected and MCP Apps render through the normal fallback path instead of the app bridge.

### Actual

  - Verified locally with system-monitor, QR, and PDF MCP Apps examples.
  - Verified upstream image comparison: upstream can call the MCP servers but does not render the MCP Apps UI resources.
  - Verified restart/reload behavior for the PDF app view.

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/aaa93975-4406-493e-a060-4ac64763704b

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:
    - No migration is required for existing installs. MCP Apps support is default-off.
    - To enable it, set:
      ```bash
      openclaw config set mcp.apps.enabled true --strict-json
      ```
    - Configure MCP servers that expose MCP Apps / `mcp-ui` resources.
    - Restart OpenClaw so the updated config is loaded.

## Risks and Mitigations

  - Risk: Enabling MCP Apps allows configured MCP servers to provide HTML/JS UI that renders inside OpenClaw.
    - Mitigation: Feature is opt-in via `mcp.apps.enabled`; app UI renders in a sandboxed iframe; docs and security audit call out the trust boundary.
  - Risk: App views can call app-visible MCP tools/resources through the bridge, increasing the practical surface area of a trusted MCP server.
    - Mitigation: Bridge is scoped to MCP app methods only; model-only tools are blocked from app bridge calls; app-only tools are hidden from the model.
  - Risk: Restored chat views may need to reconnect to MCP runtimes after a container restart.
    - Mitigation: Gateway proxy lazily recreates the session MCP runtime from config when an app bridge call arrives and no runtime is currently active.
  - Risk: Servers without MCP Apps support, or installs with the feature disabled, should not regress.
    - Mitigation: Existing MCP tool execution path remains unchanged; UI falls back to the existing embed/canvas rendering behavior when MCP Apps are disabled or metadata is absent.

target.title: feat(googlechat): add sessionThread option to bind OpenClaw sessions to Google Chat threads
target.body: AI-assisted: yes (Claude Opus 4.7). ## Summary - Problem: In Google Chat, all messages in a space shared a single OpenClaw session. Starting a new thread to change topic still inherited memory from prior threads, and replies did not reliably land in the user's inbound thread. - Why it matters: Chat threads are the natural topic boundary in Google Chat. Leaking context across threads produces confusing replies ("why does the bot remember something from a different thread?"), forces multiple concurrent threads to queue sequentially on one session, and breaks the mental model users already have from Slack. Requested in #13373. - What changed: Added an opt-in `sessionThread` channel option. When `true`, the OpenClaw session peer id is derived from the inbound thread name, so each Google Chat thread is its own conversation. Replies are now threaded via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so bot responses land in the user's thread. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Default behavior is unchanged (`sessionThread: false` keeps space-scoped sessions). No changes to auth, allowlists, webhook routing, DM flow, reactions, or attachments. No slash commands / `/focus`-style bindings added (see "future work" below). ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #13373 (per-thread session mode feature request) - Closes #41788 (Google Chat replies creating new threads instead of staying in the existing one) - Related #44347 — resolves the first of the two requests in that issue - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ### Note on naming vs the issue's proposal The issue proposes `threadSessionMode: "per-thread" | "per-space"`. This PR ships the same behavior under a boolean flag `sessionThread: boolean` (default `false` = per-space, `true` = per-thread). Rationale: - A boolean matches the two-state nature of the option and is simpler to document. - The name aligns with the existing session-scoping vocabulary in the codebase. - Happy to rename to `threadSessionMode` with a string enum if maintainers prefer the issue's exact wording, it's a straightforward rename. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: The Google Chat channel used the space id as the session peer id and did not pass the inbound thread name back on outbound messages. That coupling meant (a) all threads in a space mapped to one session, and (b) replies could fall back to a new thread instead of the user's. - Missing detection / guardrail: No peer-id unit coverage for Google Chat; Slack already has `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` as prior art for this exact pattern, but nothing equivalent existed for Google Chat. - Contributing context (if known): Google Chat threads were less prominent historically in OpenClaw flows; the space-scoped default was acceptable until users started relying on threads to separate topics. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: `extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` returns the thread name when `sessionThread` is enabled and an inbound thread is present; falls back to `spaceId` otherwise; and produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions do not cross. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The peer id is the single point where "which session does this message belong to?" is decided for Google Chat. Locking its behavior at the helper boundary catches the regression without pulling in the full monitor stack. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): None for Google Chat. Slack's `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` covers the analogous primitive. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - New opt-in config: `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` (default `false`). - `false` (default): space-scoped sessions, prior behavior unchanged. - `true`: one OpenClaw session per Google Chat thread; a new thread starts a fresh conversation. - Outbound messages now consistently target the inbound thread via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so replies land in the user's thread more reliably. This applies regardless of `sessionThread`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text sessionThread: false (default, unchanged) space S, thread A -> session(peer=S) space S, thread B -> session(peer=S) # shared memory sessionThread: true space S, thread A -> session(peer=A) space S, thread B -> session(peer=B) # isolated memory space S, no thread -> session(peer=S) # falls back to space-scoped ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No (same Chat API endpoints; only request payload now sets `thread.name` and `messageReplyOption`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No (in fact, `sessionThread: true` narrows memory scope per thread) ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: Node 22 / local gateway (`pnpm openclaw gateway restart`) - Model/provider: any (provider-agnostic) - Integration/channel (if any): Google Chat - Relevant config (redacted): ```jsonc { "channels": { "googlechat": { "enabled": true, "serviceAccountFile": "/path/to/service-account.json", "sessionThread": true } } } ``` ### Steps 1. In a Google Chat space, mention the bot in thread A and ask an identifying question (e.g. "remember the color red"). 2. Start a new thread B in the same space and ask "what color did I mention?". 3. Reply again inside thread A and ask the same question. ### Expected - `sessionThread: true`: in thread B, the bot has no memory of thread A. In thread A, the bot still remembers "red". Replies land in the thread the user messaged in. - `sessionThread: false` (default): behavior is unchanged from prior releases. ### Actual - Matches expected for both modes. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after ``` pnpm test extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is disabled ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is undefined (default) ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns thread name when sessionThread is enabled and inbound has a thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId falls back to spaceId when sessionThread is enabled but inbound has no thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions don't cross Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 5 passed (5) ``` - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - When disabled(previous behavior): - When enabled: - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: - Manually verified in a real Google Chat space against a local gateway: - Bot replies land inside the user's inbound thread (both existing threads and thread-starter messages). - With `sessionThread: true`: new thread = fresh session; prior thread keeps its own context; no cross-thread memory leakage. - With `sessionThread: false`: unchanged, space-scoped memory. - Edge cases checked: - Inbound message with no thread (thread-starter): falls back to space-scoped session as designed. - Two concurrent threads in the same space maintain separate sessions. - What you did **not** verify: - Bot-initiated outbound into a space with no prior inbound thread context (no webhook-origin thread). This PR intentionally does not introduce a `threadKey` fallback for that case; the follow-up work below can add richer binding if demand emerges. - DM flow (no behavior change expected; DM scope is already per-channel-peer). ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes - Config/env changes? Yes, additive only: new optional `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` boolean (default `false`). - Migration needed? No. Existing configs continue to behave as before. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A. To opt in, set `channels.googlechat.sessionThread: true`. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Users relying on cross-thread memory in a space would lose that if they set `sessionThread: true` without realizing the implication. - Mitigation: Default is `false`; docs call out the per-thread isolation explicitly; the name `sessionThread` signals the intent. - Risk: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` is a new exported helper surface from the plugin's `monitor.ts`. - Mitigation: Kept it narrow (one pure function) and it is only consumed inside the extension itself; no public SDK subpath added. ## Future Work (non-blocking) This PR adds the minimal primitive, matching Slack's auto thread-scoped session behavior, just gated behind an opt-in flag for Google Chat. A follow-up could align with the Telegram/Discord `session.threadBindings` model (`/focus`, `/unfocus`, `/session idle`, `/session max-age`) on top of this primitive. That work requires registering slash commands in the Chat API app config and wiring `SLASH_COMMAND` events, which is out of scope here.

target.title: Fix delivery-ready verification blockers
target.body:
## Summary

This PR closes the remaining delivery-ready verification blockers for the current branch.

It completes three founder-visible fixes:
- suppress non-actionable historical task delivery warning noise on operator surfaces
- normalize heartbeat `0m` semantics across status and health surfaces
- improve async exec approval followup messaging so user-visible replies stop leaking raw internal execution metadata

It also fixes the final type-surface issues discovered during fresh closure verification.

## What changed

### Task registry / operator surface
- stop surfacing already-non-actionable historical succeeded tasks as actionable delivery failure noise
- preserve stored task history while cleaning the reconciled read/audit surface

### Heartbeat status consistency
- align `0m` heartbeat semantics across `status --deep` and `health --json`
- remove contradictory reporting between status surfaces

### Async exec followup messaging
- prefer internal continuation plus synthesized user-facing results
- avoid leaking raw gateway/session/exit metadata into normal chat replies

### Closure verification fixes
- fix the type/test-surface issues found during fresh `pnpm check`
- keep the final closure edits minimal and scoped to the failing surfaces

## Verification

Fresh verification on this branch:
- `pnpm lint` ✅
- `pnpm check` ✅
- `pnpm build` ✅

Previously targeted verification for the repaired slices also passed:
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts` ✅
- `pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.agents.config.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-approval-followup.test.ts` ✅
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/commands/health.command.coverage.test.ts src/commands/status-all/diagnosis.test.ts` ✅
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/config/doc-baseline.integration.test.ts test/release-check.test.ts test/openclaw-npm-postpublish-verify.test.ts` ✅

## Notes

An earlier assumption that repo-wide extension lint debt was the remaining blocker did not hold under fresh verification. The real final blockers were a small set of type-surface failures, which are fixed here.

target.title: fix(gateway): iOS Talk treats SecretRef-backed API keys as missing
target.body:
﻿Closes #98209
Related: #91020

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where iOS native Talk users could see `Voice API key missing` for ElevenLabs when the Gateway was configured with `talk.providers.elevenlabs.apiKey` as a SecretRef. The failure happens because scoped `talk.config({ includeSecrets: true })` exposed the source-shaped provider key to native clients, while iOS only treats `talk.resolved.config.apiKey` as configured when it is a string.

## Why This Change Was Made

The Gateway now resolves the active Talk provider `apiKey` only for callers that already passed the `operator.talk.secrets` or admin scope check, and only feeds that materialized value into the runtime-resolved Talk config path. Ordinary read-scope callers still use the existing strip/redact path and do not receive resolved credential material.

The provider map contract stays source-shaped: `talk.providers.<id>.apiKey` remains the authored SecretRef, while `talk.resolved.config.apiKey` is the runtime string for authorized native clients. A prior attempt (#91020) touched the same early-return seam but raised the credential-boundary concern; this version keeps the fix narrow and relies on the documented `operator.talk.secrets` native-read scope.

## User Impact

Users who pair the iOS app with Talk secret scope can use SecretRef-backed ElevenLabs native Talk configuration without copying the provider key into plaintext config or entering a separate local mobile key. Users without Talk secret scope continue to receive redacted config and cannot read the resolved key.

## Evidence

Issue proof:

- #98209 records the Windows/iOS incident: OpenClaw `2026.6.11-beta.2`, iOS paired successfully, ElevenLabs native Talk showed `Voice API key missing`, and the same key became usable only after a plaintext local workaround.
- Source-level before proof on current `main` (`6cb82eaab865`): `resolveTalkResponseFromConfig` returned early for `includeSecrets`, so runtime Talk provider resolution was skipped.
- iOS source proof: `TalkModeGatewayConfigParser` reads `activeConfig["apiKey"]?.stringValue`, so a SecretRef object in `talk.resolved.config.apiKey` is treated as missing.

Live Gateway proof on this branch (`15d77f65287e`):

I started the current branch Gateway with a temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`/`OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH` and a fake env SecretRef only, then called `talk.config` over a live Gateway WebSocket using the Gateway client. The command exited 0.

```json
{
  "command": "live gateway run + GatewayClient talk.config RPC",
  "head": "15d77f65287e",
  "config": "temporary OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH with fake env SecretRef only",
  "scopedIncludeSecrets": {
    "providerMapApiKeyShape": "SecretRef",
    "providerMapApiKeyId": "OC_PROOF_ELEVEN_API_KEY",
    "resolvedApiKeyType": "string",
    "resolvedApiKeyRedacted": "<resolved fake env key>",
    "resolvedVoiceId": "voice-proof"
  },
  "readScope": {
    "providerMapApiKeyShape": "SecretRef",
    "providerMapApiKeyId": "__OPENCLAW_REDACTED__",
    "resolvedApiKeyShape": "SecretRef",
    "resolvedApiKeyId": "__OPENCLAW_REDACTED__",
    "leakedFakeKey": false
  }
}
```

This proves the current-head Gateway response has the intended split contract: scoped native clients receive a string under `talk.resolved.config.apiKey`, while unscoped/read callers do not receive the resolved key.

After-fix local test proof:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server.talk-config.test.ts --testNamePattern="gateway talk.config"
```

Result: `Test Files 2 passed (2)`, `Tests 20 passed (20)`. This real Gateway WebSocket integration coverage proves `talk.providers.<id>.apiKey` stays SecretRef-shaped, `talk.resolved.config.apiKey` is the resolved env value for scoped `includeSecrets` callers, and ordinary read-scope callers still receive redacted SecretRef fields.

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/talk.test.ts --testNamePattern="talk.config"
```

Result: `Test Files 2 passed (2)`, `Tests 6 passed | 60 skipped (66)`.

```text
pnpm exec oxlint src/gateway/server-methods/talk.ts src/gateway/server-methods/talk.test.ts src/gateway/server.talk-config.test.ts
git diff --check
```

Result: both exited cleanly.

```text
$env:PYTHONUTF8='1'; python .agents\skills\autoreview\scripts\autoreview --mode local --engine codex --thinking medium --stream-engine-output
```

Result: `autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported`; `overall: patch is correct (0.86)`.

What was not tested: no physical iOS app session against this branch and no live ElevenLabs synthesis request. The proof uses fake credential strings only; no real API key is included in the issue, PR body, tests, or commit.

target.title: fix(systemd): reconcile managed-env unit migrations
target.body:
## Summary

- carry `#66295` onto current `main`
- fix the two real migration regressions from review: rebuild malformed units when `ExecStart=` is missing, and reconcile stale `WorkingDirectory=` in-place instead of leaving `CHDIR` failures behind
- remove the redundant managed-env split and clean up the temp-home test leakage

Fixes #66248.
Related #66219.
Supersedes #66295.

## Why this carry exists

`#66295` had the right core direction: move OpenClaw-managed systemd env into a drop-in so upgrades stop clobbering user `EnvironmentFile=` and `Environment=` directives.

The branch still needed maintainer follow-up in three spots:

1. malformed existing units with no `ExecStart=` were no longer repaired
2. stale `WorkingDirectory=` values could survive migration and fail startup at `CHDIR`
3. the new integration tests were leaving temp dirs behind

This carry fixes those and keeps the user-preserving drop-in design intact.

## Verification

- `pnpm test:serial src/daemon/systemd-unit.test.ts src/daemon/systemd.test.ts`
- `pnpm build`

## Changelog

Added under `## Unreleased` with:
- `Thanks @solomonneas.`
- `Carry follow-up: @vincentkoc.`
